title: Who is using AI to code? Global diffusion and impact of generative AI
abstract: Generative coding tools promise big productivity gains, but uneven uptake could widen skill and income gaps. We train a neural classifier to spot artificial intelligence (AI)-generated Python functions in more than 30 million GitHub commits by 160,097 software developers, tracking how fast, and where, these tools take hold. Currently, AI writes an estimated 29% of Python functions in the US-a shrinking lead over other countries. We estimate that quarterly output, measured in online code contributions, consequently increased by 3.6%. AI seems to benefit experienced, senior-level developers: They increased productivity and more readily expanded into new domains of software development. By contrast, early-career developers showed no significant benefits from AI adoption. This may widen skill gaps and reshape future career ladders in software development.
summary_en: Who actually uses AI to code? This paper trains a neural classifier to detect AI-generated Python functions across 30 million+ GitHub commits by 160,000+ developers, and estimates global AI-coding diffusion. Key findings: AI currently writes about 29% of Python functions in the US, a shrinking lead over other countries. Quarterly output (measured in online code contributions) rose by 3.6% from AI adoption. But: AI benefits experienced, senior developers — they increased productivity and expanded into new domains. Early-career developers showed no significant benefits. The paper warns this may widen skill gaps and reshape software-engineering career ladders.
data_used: GitHub commit data from 30+ million commits by 160,097 developers across multiple programming languages. Neural classifier trained on labelled human-AI code pairs to detect AI-generated functions.
policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian IT-sector policy, digital-skills training, and Industry 4.0 programs. The paper implies that AI-productivity support should target junior developers explicitly (who are not currently benefiting); and that GDP measurement based on IT-sector output understates actual productivity gains. Relevant to NGM digital-strategy design, ITM training programs, and university CS-curriculum reform. Caveat: Python-only measurement; broader software-development patterns may differ.
